article.title: LHCb Completes the Doubly Charmed Baryon Doublet With First Particle Discovery From Its Upgraded Detector
article.summary: CERN's LHCb experiment has observed the doubly charmed baryon Xicc+, a heavy cousin of the proton containing two charm quarks and one down quark, completing an isospin doublet first opened by the 2017 discovery of its partner particle.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

The LHCb collaboration at CERN has announced the discovery of a new subatomic particle, a doubly charmed baryon designated Ξcc⁺ (Xi-cc-plus), composed of two charm quarks and one down quark. The particle, roughly four times heavier than a proton, was presented at the Rencontres de Moriond Electroweak conference on March 17 and represents the first new particle identified using the upgraded LHCb detector, according to [CERN](https://home.cern/news/news/physics/lhcb-collaboration-discovers-new-proton-particle).

The observation completes an isospin doublet that began with LHCb's 2017 discovery of the closely related Ξcc⁺⁺, which contains two charm quarks and one up quark. Together, the two particles form a matched pair that transforms doubly charmed baryons from isolated curiosities into what physicists describe as a precision laboratory for studying how the strong force binds matter, as reported by [CERN EP News](https://ep-news.web.cern.ch/content/doubly-charmed-baryon-doublet-completed-upgraded-lhcb).

## What We Know

The Ξcc⁺ was detected in proton-proton collision data collected during 2024 at a center-of-mass energy of 13.6 TeV, using approximately 6.9 inverse femtobarns of data from LHC Run 3. Physicists identified the particle through its decay into three lighter particles — Λc⁺, K⁻, and π⁺ — observing a clear signal peak of approximately 915 events, according to [ScienceDaily](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/03/260319005106.htm).

The measured mass of 3,619.97 MeV/c² is consistent with theoretical predictions and closely matches the 3,621 MeV mass of its 2017 partner particle, as expected for isospin partners where the only difference is a down quark substituted for an up quark. The statistical significance of the signal exceeds 7 sigma, well above the 5 sigma threshold conventionally required for a discovery claim in particle physics, according to [CERN](https://home.cern/news/news/physics/lhcb-collaboration-discovers-new-proton-particle).

The result also resolves a scientific debate that stretched back more than 20 years. A previous experiment had reported a possible sighting of a similar doubly charmed state, but the observation was never confirmed and its reported properties did not match theoretical expectations. The new measurement, with its high statistical significance and mass consistent with predictions, settles the question, as reported by [ScienceDaily](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/03/260319005106.htm).

The discovery was made possible by the LHCb detector's major upgrade, completed in 2023, which involved contributions from more than 1,000 scientists across 20 countries. Scientists at the University of Manchester designed and built silicon pixel detector modules that function as a high-speed camera capable of imaging particles 40 million times per second, according to [Phys.org](https://phys.org/news/2026-03-scientists-play-key-role-discovery.html). The upgrade represented the UK's largest national contribution to the project.

"This discovery showcases the extraordinary capability of the upgraded LHCb detector and the strength of UK and Manchester contributions," said Professor Chris Parkes, head of Physics and Astronomy at the University of Manchester, as quoted by [Phys.org](https://phys.org/news/2026-03-scientists-play-key-role-discovery.html).

The finding brings the total number of hadrons discovered by LHC experiments to 80, according to [CERN](https://home.cern/news/news/physics/lhcb-collaboration-discovers-new-proton-particle).

## What We Don't Know

Despite the clear discovery signal, several properties of the Ξcc⁺ remain to be measured. Theoretical models predict its lifetime could be up to six times shorter than that of the Ξcc⁺⁺, but a direct lifetime measurement will require significantly larger data samples than are currently available, according to [CERN EP News](https://ep-news.web.cern.ch/content/doubly-charmed-baryon-doublet-completed-upgraded-lhcb).

The precise mass splitting between the two isospin partners also needs refinement. While the current measurement is consistent with theoretical expectations, the short expected lifetime of the Ξcc⁺ introduces uncertainties that larger datasets should help resolve.

Beyond the doublet, physicists are now searching for additional members of the doubly heavy baryon family, including the Ωcc⁺ (containing two charm quarks and a strange quark), whose lifetime is predicted to fall between those of the two Ξcc states. The ultimate prize would be a triple-charm baryon, though no such particle has been observed.

## Analysis

The completion of the Ξcc doublet marks a significant step for quantum chromodynamics, the theory governing the strong nuclear force. Having two particles with nearly identical masses but different quark compositions allows physicists to test predictions about how charm quarks interact within baryons with unprecedented precision.

The LHCb collaboration expects to collect thousands of doubly charmed baryons as data-taking continues through Run 3 and into the planned High-Luminosity LHC era, enabling precision studies of lifetimes, branching fractions, and production mechanisms, as reported by [CERN EP News](https://ep-news.web.cern.ch/content/doubly-charmed-baryon-doublet-completed-upgraded-lhcb). The upgraded detector's ability to process 40 million collision images per second, combined with custom silicon chips that have applications in medical imaging, positions LHCb to remain at the forefront of hadron spectroscopy for years to come.
